description: Control systems theory - feedback, stability, analysis.

facts.context: The integral term (I) in a PID controller eliminates:
facts.truth: steady-state error in the system response
distractors: oscillations in the transient response
overshoot during step response completely
the need for proportional control term

facts.truth: encirclements of -1 by open-loop frequency response
distractors: pole locations in the s-plane directly
step response overshoot percentage only
gain value at the crossover frequency

facts.truth: all internal states can be determined from outputs
distractors: all states are controllable from inputs directly
the system is guaranteed stable in closed-loop
transfer function has no right-half-plane zeros
only modifies the system steady-state gain
cannot stabilize an unstable open-loop system
requires output feedback rather than state feedback

facts.context: Gain margin is measured in dB at the:
facts.truth: phase crossover frequency where phase is -180°
distractors: gain crossover frequency where gain is 0 dB
resonant frequency where gain peaks maximum
bandwidth frequency where gain drops by 3 dB

facts.truth: checking signs in first column of Routh array
distractors: calculating all pole locations numerically
simulating step response until steady state
measuring phase margin from Bode plot

facts.context: The derivative term (D) in PID provides:
distractors: elimination of steady-state error completely
increased response to constant setpoint changes
noise filtering for measurement signals

facts.truth: any state reachable from any initial state via inputs
distractors: the output can be measured without noise
all poles are in the left half-plane
the transfer function is strictly proper

facts.truth: magnitude and phase versus frequency on log scale
distractors: pole and zero locations in complex plane
step response amplitude versus time
state trajectories in phase space

facts.context: Root locus shows closed-loop pole locations as:
facts.truth: gain K varies from zero to infinity
distractors: frequency varies across the spectrum
time constant changes with temperature
plant parameters drift over system lifetime

facts.context: Kalman filter provides optimal state estimation when:
facts.truth: noise is Gaussian and system is linear
distractors: no noise is present in measurements at all
the system is nonlinear with any noise type
measurements are received without any delay

facts.context: Phase margin is measured at the:
facts.truth: gain crossover frequency where magnitude is 0 dB
distractors: phase crossover where phase equals -180 degrees
resonant peak frequency of the closed-loop
DC frequency where gain is maximum
